content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 307 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95814-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 9, 2000


David H. Johnson
6223 Avenida Ganso
Goleta, California 93117

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-090

Dear Mr. Johnson:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. After you retire from the City of Santa Barbara as Public Works Director, can you
accept employment with an engineering firm which contracts with the City?

2. Is there a period of time that must pass following your retirement before you can
accept a position with a firm which contracts with the City?

3. Would you be able to work on new City projects for which contracts were issued after
you left employment with the City?

4. Are there any specific prohibitions that would preclude you from working for an
engineering firm that contracts with the City?


¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-00-090
Page 2

CONCLUSION

The Act's "revolving door" provisions, which prohibit a former public official from
taking part in certain proceedings in which the official participated while in government service
and which prohibit a former public official from appearing before or communicating with his or
her former agency for one year, apply only to "state administrative officials" or to designated
employees of "state administrative agencies." (Sections 87400-87406.)

In your capacity as a city employee, you are a public official of a local governmental
agency. Therefore, the revolving door provisions of the Act do not apply to you. However, you
may wish to consult with the City of Santa Barbara to ascertain if the city has a local ordinance
regarding revolving door provisions for former employees.

FACTS

For the past 27 years, you have been employed by the City of Santa Barbara, serving as
City Engineer and for the last 17 years as Public Works Director. You are planning your
retirement from the City and are considering options for employment. While working for the
City, you would not pursue any employment opportunities that could be influenced by your
present position nor would you accept a position in the future which necessitated your
involvement with any City project which fell within the scope of your responsibilities while you
served as Public Works Director.

ANALYSIS

The Act's "revolving door" provisions, which prohibit a former public official from
taking part in certain proceedings in which the official participated while in government service
and which prohibit a former public official from appearing before or communicating with his or
her former agency for one year, apply only to "state administrative officials" or to designated
employees of "state administrative agencies." (Sections 87400-87406; Moore Advice Letter,
No. I-99-051.)

Please note, however, that our advice is limited to the Act. You may wish to contact the
Attorney General's Office with respect to other provisions of law, such as the law governing
incompatible public offices and Government Code Section 1090.

File No. A-00-090
Page 3


If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.


Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

[signature]

By: Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant
